why: Closest full-package match: Mediterranean south coast, wine districts, wheat–sheep belt, orchard valleys, and a strong coastal-to-inland gradient.
Mediterranean valley-and-coast agriculture with wine, fruit, cereals, and sheep; somewhat cooler and more summer-rainfall influenced in parts.
Southwest corner has a classic wheat–sheep belt plus wine and fruit; flatter and more spatially extensive than the Western Cape.
Mediterranean fruit-and-wine region with valley farming and surrounding uplands; less maritime and less sheep-oriented than the Cape.
Especially the Central Coast/Central Valley mix: Mediterranean climate, vineyards, fruit, and irrigated field crops; much larger and more irrigation-heavy.
Mediterranean coastal hills with wine, fruit, and vegetables; less cereal/sheep weight than the Cape interior.
Dry Mediterranean with vineyards, wheat, and sheep/goats; hotter and more olive-dominant.
Rolling dry Mediterranean grain–sheep–wine landscape; flatter and more inland than the Western Cape.
Strong cereal-livestock similarity and Mediterranean dryness; weaker coastal and fruit-valley analogue.
Mediterranean vineyards, citrus, wheat, and sheep; island setting and heat regime are stronger than in the Western Cape.
Very good sheep–wine–grain analogue in a Mediterranean setting; less orchard-valley emphasis.
Mediterranean coast-plus-hills with vineyards, citrus, and grazing; more olive-heavy and generally hotter.
Wine, wheat, olive, and hill-country agriculture fit well; somewhat cooler/wetter and more forested.
Mediterranean coast, vineyards, fruit, and cereals; more urban-industrial and less sheep-centered.
Mediterranean wine belt with cereal and sheep zones in the hinterland; less comparable dryland grain structure.
Mediterranean fruit and viticulture in coastal valleys and foothills; weaker wheat–sheep match.
Mediterranean fruit/citrus and wine zone; more irrigation- and citrus-weighted than the Western Cape.
Aegean Mediterranean interior with grapes, olives, cereals, and livestock; less maritime-cool and less wheat-sheep balanced.
Aegean coastal vineyards, fruit, and mixed farming; more urban and more olive/citrus oriented.
Useful for dry Mediterranean horticulture and vineyards; too arid and irrigation-dependent for a full analogue.
Best broad analogue if one includes the High Plains, brush country, and irrigated river belts: cattle, sorghum/maize, cotton, and citrus all coexist.
Excellent Northern Cape-type dry plateau analogue with ranching and irrigated valleys; too thin on the North West maize/groundnut side.
Arid ranching plateau with river irrigation and mixed livestock; generally drier and less grain-heavy.
Semi-arid cattle/sheep country with irrigation pockets and grape production; weaker summer-rainfall grain component.
Strong match for the grassier ranching-and-grain margin of the combined region; cooler and less desert-fringe.
Very good for the arid interior plus irrigated fruit/vineyard side; less maize/groundnut and more Andean irrigation dependence.
Dry interior grazing and crop-margin analogue with plains and basins; less irrigation horticulture.
Arid cattle country with irrigated wheat/cotton/citrus; too hot and too Pacific-desert leaning.
Dry plateau cereals, sheep, and vineyards fit well; Mediterranean seasonality differs from the summer-rainfall side.
Semi-arid interior with cereals, sheep, and irrigated fruit zones; cooler and more winter-moist.
Strong dry-plateau grain–sheep analogue; weaker cattle-ranch and river-irrigation fruit element.
Steppe plateau with cereals and livestock; less arid-western and less irrigated-fruit oriented.
Useful for semi-arid livestock and millet/grain systems; hotter and less like the North West maize/groundnut belt.
Dry plateau with sheep/cattle and irrigation pockets; weaker maize and subtropical crop component.
Captures the drier pastoral Northern Cape end well; too arid and too sparse for the North West side.
Desert-basin irrigation plus livestock is a strong structural match; much colder in winter and more continental.
Steppe ranching and grain margins are useful analogues; climate is too cold and continental.
Interior pastoralism plus wheat belt and irrigation corridors fit parts of the system; Mediterranean south coast skews the state overall.
Wheat–sheep belt and arid interior match some Northern Cape traits; rainfall seasonality is not close.
Useful mainly for the North West-type cattle-and-grain margin; the tropical north dilutes the fit.
Best all-round analogue: coast, tablelands, and dry interior support sheep, beef, dairy, grains, and fruit in a very Eastern Cape-like mix.
Strong livestock, dairy, grain, and fruit analogue with temperate coast-to-inland contrast; generally cooler and wetter.
Excellent for sheep, beef, dairy, grain, and an east-coast dry plain backed by higher country; much cooler overall.
Good cattle–sheep–grain analogue with temperate to subtropical coasts and rolling interior; less dry Karoo-like country.
Very strong for sheep/beef high country and dry east-coast valleys; colder and less subtropical on the coast.
Livestock, wheat, and dairy fit very well; too flat and lacking the escarpment/Karoo split.
Coastal livestock and mixed farming resemble the softer eastern coastal side; lacks the dry interior and mountain edge.
Cattle, grains, and citrus fit well; too humid and riverine.
Rolling cattle-and-sheep country with crop pockets; weaker coast and drier interior analogue.
Mixed grain, fruit, and livestock region on the Mediterranean–temperate transition; less wool/mohair weighted.
Useful for coast plus upland farming and livestock; more forestry-heavy and wetter.
Hillier sheep-and-cattle country than much of Uruguay; still lacks the Karoo and Indian Ocean coast contrast.
Works for mixed grain, fruit, and livestock agriculture; too Mediterranean and not enough humid summer-rain zone.
Coastal cattle and dairy resemblance is good; far too wet and not sheep/Karoo enough.
Useful for rugged coast plus dairy/beef systems; weak analogue for the interior dry-stock side.
Sheep, dairy, beef, and fruit fit well in parts; climate is too cool and maritime.
Livestock-heavy and pastoral, with some grain; too cool and lacking the drier inland contrast.
Strong coastal livestock and dairy system; almost no analogue for the semi-arid interior.
Useful for cattle and subtropical crop pockets; far too humid and low-lying.
Good mixed-farming plateau with cattle and grains; weaker sheep/Karoo analogue and less coastal similarity.
Hot brush-country plus irrigated citrus and cattle system is one of the closest agricultural matches.
Especially South Texas and adjoining interior belts: cattle, citrus, sorghum/maize, and hot savanna-to-semi-arid conditions.
Warm cattle-and-grain savanna with some citrus and diversified agriculture; more mechanized and wetter in many districts.
Hot lowland cattle, sugar, maize, and fruit region with bushland and frontier agriculture; flatter and more humid in the core.
Warm plateau agriculture with cattle, maize, and irrigated horticulture; less citrus/subtropical-fruit centered.
Vidarbha/Marathwada-type hot inland farming with citrus, cotton, grains, and livestock resembles parts of Limpopo.
Semi-arid heat, cattle, groundnuts, cotton, and citrus make it a useful analogue; coastal and monsoonal structure differs.
Hot plateau with maize, cotton, horticulture, and livestock; weaker citrus and bushveld resemblance.
Subtropical cattle-and-crop province with hot conditions and some fruit; flatter and wetter.
Hot cattle-and-grain/cotton lowland analogue; less subtropical-fruit emphasis.
Useful for cattle, hot dry tropics, irrigation horticulture, and upland-to-lowland contrasts; too large and too coastal in aggregate.
Diverse warm uplands with cattle, maize, fruit, and tobacco; generally less bushveld and less semi-arid.
Hot cattle-and-citrus zone with dry-forest conditions; too flat and lacking Limpopo’s upland crop belt.
Warm cattle and mixed tropical farming; too wet/lowland.
Hot savanna cattle-and-grain state with river valleys; less horticultural and less topographically varied.
Strong citrus and tropical-crop analogue, but much too humid and coastal.
Warm mixed farming with citrus, rice, and livestock; too monsoonal and less bushveld-like.
Dry tropical mixed farming with irrigation and cattle gives a partial analogue; crop package differs substantially.
Useful for hot dry-season cattle country; much weaker citrus/maize and less subtropical.
Hot irrigated horticultural state with cattle and maize; too irrigation-dominant and coastal.
Excellent for the sugarcane–citrus–cattle–plateau mix; less rugged and less forestry-heavy than Mpumalanga plus Eswatini.
Very strong humid plateau analogue with maize, cattle, forestry, and citrus; sugarcane is important but not as dominant as in the lowveld.
Cane, cattle, citrus, and escarpment/coastal gradients make it a very strong structural match; less maize-plateau identity.
Humid hills and plateau farming with livestock and forestry fit well; weaker sugarcane analogue.
Varied upland farming with cattle, forestry, citrus, and maize; sugarcane less central and relief pattern broader.
Red-soil subtropical hills with forestry, citrus, and cattle are very comparable; little large-scale sugarcane.
Humid uplands and piedmont with forestry, livestock, and row crops make a good broad analogue; lacks sugarcane.
Warm humid farming with forestry and livestock fits parts of the system; less escarpment and less cane.
Good for humid mixed farming and forestry; weaker highland component.
One of the best cane-and-citrus hill-country analogues globally; rice is more important and cattle less so.
Altitude gradients, tobacco, cane in warmer belts, and forestry fit well; more upland and more diversified than Mpumalanga.
Warm hilly farming with cane, citrus, beef, and forestry is a notable partial analogue; island/volcanic setting differs.
Subtropical hills, citrus, and forestry fit; sugarcane and maize are less central.
Cattle, rice, and citrus in warm humid lowlands resemble the lowveld side; weak escarpment match.
Coast-to-hill gradient with forestry, cattle, and citrus; less maize and less cane.
Useful for cane, citrus, cattle, and elevation contrasts; generally hotter and less temperate-highveld.
Foothill gradient with maize, sugarcane, tobacco, and citrus gives a surprisingly good agricultural analogue.
Foothill forests plus sugarcane and citrus fit some belts; too Himalayan and less lowveld-sugar oriented.
Mountain-to-lowland gradient with cane, citrus, cattle, and forestry offers a useful partial match.
Humid mountainous citrus-and-forest region; much weaker cattle and maize resemblance.
Best overall analogue for open plains, grain orientation, cattle, and the balance between dryland and better-watered districts.
Strong maize–wheat–cattle match on open plains; generally more productive and more irrigation-intensive.
Excellent summer-rain grain-and-cattle analogue, especially for maize and wheat; lower altitude and more intensive row cropping.
Very strong for drier grain-livestock plains and sheep/cattle balance; cooler and flatter.
Plains grain-and-cattle mix with some wheat specialization; warmer and more variable.
Useful for elevated plains, cattle, and wheat; much colder and drier.
Large-scale wheat, maize, beef, and dairy fit well; too humid and too flat overall.
Excellent broad grain-belt analogue with cattle; far colder and more continental.
Prairie grain and beef fit well; colder and less maize-oriented.
Darling Downs-type grain and cattle belt is a good partial analogue; warmer and more variable.
Interior wheat–sheep–cattle belts fit well; strong coastal share makes the state broader than the analogue.
Open lowland cereals and livestock provide a solid European analogue; winter-cold regime differs.
Another strong plain-grain-livestock analogue; less like the elevated Highveld physiography.
Cereal and livestock plain with some irrigation; less summer-rainfall shaped.
Steppe grain-belt analogue with cattle; much colder and more continental.
Broad open grain steppe fit is good; temperature regime diverges sharply.
Very good for steppe grain structure; weak maize and grazing-climate equivalence.
Useful for steppe grain and livestock; colder and more continental.
Grassland–grain transition and livestock fit well; winter severity is much greater.
Grain and livestock on an open plain work as a lower-tier analogue; rainfall seasonality and maritime influence differ.
High-altitude urban-industrial region with peri-urban maize, dairy, and horticulture: probably the closest broad functional analogue.
High plateau around a dominant metropolis with dairying, vegetables, and mixed farming on the urban fringe.
Highland industrial state with a major metro, maize, dairy, horticulture, and strong service/manufacturing sectors.
Compact industrial highland region with expanding metro agriculture and manufacturing, similar in economic role if smaller.
Urban-industrial corridor on a temperate plateau with peri-urban farming; less mining-centered.
Highland capital region with large service economy and an agricultural fringe of grains and cattle; more planned and less mining-linked.
Bengaluru plateau gives a strong urban-highland analogue, with dairy and horticulture nearby; monsoonal crop system differs.
Big inland metro on a high plateau with surrounding cereals and livestock; rainfall seasonality and Mediterranean regime differ.
Urban-political core on an interior plateau with surrounding grain/livestock agriculture; drier and more continental.
Hyderabad-centered plateau economy with peri-urban horticulture and dairying; hotter and less grassland-like.
Dense urban-industrial plateau core with horticulture and dairying nearby; much drier and more irrigation-dependent.
Front Range urban belt on an elevated interior with cattle/grain fringes is a good loose analogue; much colder and drier.
Urban plateau and mining-history overlap is useful; agriculture and rainfall regime are weaker matches.
Kunming-centered plateau urban region with mixed farming margins; more monsoonal and less mining-industrial in structure.
High-altitude industrial/urban core with maize and horticulture around it; more volcanic and less grassland.
Useful for mining plus highland urbanity; too dry and less metropolitan.
Peri-urban highland dairy/horticulture around a dominant city fits some Gauteng functions; much smaller scale.
Highland agriculture around a very large urban core offers a useful partial analogue; stronger cereal and less industry intensity.
Highland urban region with grazing country around it; far smaller and less industrial.
Belo Horizonte and the mining–industry–highland mix make a useful loose analogue; agriculture is broader and less peri-urban focused.
Excellent pastoral high-country analogue with sheep, rugged relief, and cool dry valleys; Lesotho is poorer and more subsistence-crop dependent.
Strong sheep-and-grain/high-country analogue, especially along the foothill and interior belts; flatter lowland share is larger.
Mountain pastoralism plus valley cropping fit well; generally drier and more Andean.
Very strong highland grazing analogue with sparse settlement and severe terrain; colder and less crop-oriented.
Rocky Mountain highland grazing with valley agriculture provides a good partial analogue; richer, colder, and less wool-focused.
High, dry mountain-and-valley pastoral country with limited cropping; hotter lower belts and less summer-rain grassland.
Harsh highland pastoralism is similar in structure; far more extreme altitude and colder temperatures.
Another strong mountain-pastoral analogue with sparse cropland; again much harsher and more Andean.
Useful mountain-to-valley analogue with mixed livestock and cereals; warmer and more diverse.
Elevated grazing country with sparse settlement fits well; much colder and less densely peasant-farmed.
Highland livestock and valley farming create a reasonable analogue; altitude and crop bundle differ.
Mountain farming and grazing fit structurally; crop set and temperature regime are more Andean.
Cool highland pastoral subsistence agriculture is similar; altitude is more extreme.
Good for mountain subsistence plus grazing; drier and more Andean in culture and crop mix.
Cold upland livestock and grain region; much more continental and winter-severe.
High plateau/mountain grazing analogue; colder and less maize-based.
Extreme mountain pastoralism matches the relief logic; far too high and arid.
Mountain grazing and valley cultivation are a good partial fit; colder and less densely rural.
High cold pastoral plateau analogue; far more extreme in altitude and climate.
Useful only for the logic of mountain enclosure and grazing dominance; too high, too cold, and too sparse for a close analogue.
Best large-scale analogue for tropical/subtropical coastal cane, cattle, citrus, and a coast-to-drier-interior transition.
Strong sugarcane–citrus–cattle coastal lowland analogue; generally wetter and more mountainous in places.
Coastal cane/cassava/coconut and inland cattle resemble southern Mozambique well; broader state and more varied climates.
Hot coast-plus-interior farming with rice, cane, citrus, and cattle gives a very good agricultural match.
Rice, cashew, coastal lowlands, and drier inland belts make a strong analogue; cattle is less central.
Excellent for cane-dominant tropical coast; too narrow and less comparable inland.
Another strong sugarcane coastal belt analogue; more humid forest history and less cattle-savanna inland.
Coastal fruit and mixed farming analogue with some cattle; less cane-heavy than southern Mozambique.
Useful for hot coastal farming, rice, sugarcane, coconut, and drier inland tracts; rainfall regime is more monsoonal and bimodal.
Tropical lowland farming with sugar, fruit, and coastal plain conditions fits well; much more humid and island-based.
Subtropical coastal agriculture with fruit, cane, and rice is comparable; urbanization is far more intense.
Useful for warm coastal horticulture and rice; weaker inland savanna-cattle match.
Citrus, sugar, and flat subtropical lowlands fit well; too developed and too wet in the peninsula core.
Sugarcane and rice on coastal lowlands give a useful partial analogue; cooler winter regime and less tropical tree-crop package.
Subtropical sugarcane and coastal farming match parts of the profile; scale and settlement pattern differ sharply.
Coastal lowland rice, cane, and mixed farming with drier inland transition fit well; cattle and citrus are less central.
Another coast-plus-interior rice/cane analogue; generally cooler north and more rice-weighted.
Warm Pacific coastal agriculture with cane, fruit, and cattle gives a decent partial fit; topography differs.
Strong warm irrigated crop analogue for the riverine plain side; too irrigation-dominant and less cassava/cashew based.
Hot coastal mixed farming fits some tropical lowland traits; much wetter and more volcanic.
Probably the best broad agricultural analogue: elevated subtropical plateau, maize, tobacco, cattle, and diversified commercial farming.
Highland mixed farming with cattle, maize, tobacco, and strong regional diversity resembles Zimbabwe well.
Tobacco, maize, cattle, rolling uplands, and a humid warm climate make this a very good analogue.
Another strong tobacco-and-cattle upland analogue; somewhat cooler and more forested.
Warm plateau with maize, cattle, and diversified field crops fits broad structure; less tobacco-centered.
Rolling tobacco-and-cattle country with maize in a humid climate; cooler and more winter-cold.
High-value agriculture, cattle, maize, and upland commercial farming fit; sugarcane/citrus are more dominant than in Zimbabwe.
Strong cattle and grain analogue with warm plateau/lowland conditions; less elevated and less tobacco-oriented.
Altitude, tobacco, maize, and diverse upland farming make this a strong non-American analogue; more mountainous and more monsoonal.
Cattle and warm-climate cropping fit some belts; much flatter and wetter.
Subtropical plateau, tobacco, and diversified farming create a useful match; cattle/maize proportions differ.
Upland mixed agriculture and climate fit partially; terrain is more rugged and less ranch-like.
Warm plateau with soy/maize/cotton and cattle gives a structural analogue; tobacco less central and monsoon stronger.
Good for diverse warm upland farming including cotton, maize, and livestock; drier and more orchard/cotton weighted.
Useful for humid upland cattle and tobacco; generally more forested and cooler.
Mixed grains, cattle, and some citrus provide a workable partial analogue; too riverine and low-lying.
Warm upland agriculture and some sugar/citrus fit special belts of Zimbabwe; too sugar-centered.
Useful mainly for cattle and warm plateau farming; weaker tobacco and elevation analogue.
Maize, tobacco, sugarcane, and elevation gradients create a noteworthy agricultural parallel; far more mountainous.
Major maize-and-cattle province, but too flat and humid for a close plateau analogue.
One of the best overall analogues for dry ranching, sparse settlement, and drought-prone interior basins and plains.
Strong desert-rangeland cattle analogue with similar sparsity and climate stress.
Very good semi-arid ranching state with similar broad production logic; irrigation plays a larger role in some districts.
Useful mainly for the western and High Plains cattle/sorghum belts; the state's humid east is not analogous.
Hot semi-arid to subhumid rangeland with cattle and sorghum-like crop systems; somewhat more humid.
Arid ranching with sparse settlement and irrigated pockets; too desert-coastal in parts.
Warm cattle-and-sorghum lowland with large extensive ranching areas; flatter and less sandy.
Hot dry rangeland and low-productivity crop areas are a good analogue; more forested.
Drought-prone livestock-and-millet state with sparse dryland farming; denser population and stronger irrigation politics.
Large dry pastoral region with sparse settlement; colder uplands and more irrigation oases.
Useful dry pastoral analogue; crop mix and temperature regime differ.
Huge arid interior with pastoral margins and irrigated oases; much colder and more continental.
Steppe pastoralism and sparse settlement fit well; winter severity is much greater.
Vast low-density cattle country fits well, especially structurally; northern tropical seasonality differs.
Useful for interior rangeland and cattle; too much humid east coast in aggregate.
Pastoral interior analogue is good; rainfall seasonality diverges.
Dry pastoral interior gives a partial analogue; crop bundle differs.
Sparse dry grazing economy with irrigation pockets; far colder and more continental.
Useful for arid sparse rangeland logic; too desert-oil oriented and not grain/millet enough.
Dry rangeland and sparse settlement fit; Botswana is generally warmer and more savanna-like.
Probably the closest broad structural analogue: savanna cattle country with sparse infrastructure and strong dry-season constraints.
Especially the northwestern and inland savanna belts: cattle, dry tropical farming, and climate variability fit well.
Miombo/savanna zone with maize/millet and cattle gives a strong African analogue.
Dry savanna agropastoralism with cattle and grains is highly comparable.
Another strong millet/maize-plus-cattle savanna analogue; slightly drier and more central-plateau in feel.
Dry agropastoral grain zone with cattle and goats; less woodland and more central-plateau.
Warm lowland cattle and grain frontier agriculture create a useful parallel; flatter and more commercially expansive.
Savanna woodland with cattle and grains fits structurally; far more mechanized and soy-dominant.
Warm cerrado cattle-and-grain state with climate resemblance in parts; less millet-oriented and more commercial.
Hot cattle-and-grain savanna state that matches broad production logic; less populated in the smallholder sense.
Strong dry-tropical livestock analogue with modest grain farming; weaker cattle scale and different vegetation.
Hot savanna cattle zone with maize in rural districts; too karstic and more humid.
Cattle and maize in a hot lowland setting provide a good partial analogue; wetter and more forested.
Maize, millet, and cattle within a savanna belt resemble northern Namibia's mixed agropastoral profile.
Cattle-oriented savanna with mixed grain farming; generally wetter and more densely settled.
Dry-zone mixed farming offers a limited analogue for the crop logic; livestock and settlement structure differ.
Hot low-productivity cattle-and-grain zone is a usable partial match; less woodland-savanna in the African sense.
Warm cattle-and-crop lowland with sparse districts; too humid and riverine.
Interior dry tropical cattle and crop belts provide a lower-tier analogue; state-level diversity is large.
Useful for hot frontier savanna/forest transition farming; generally more humid and more rice/cassava oriented.
Closest overall analogue: desert coast, mining, sparse settlement, goats/sheep in pockets, and irrigated grape/fruit valleys.
Excellent for arid basins, mining, sparse population, and extensive grazing; less coastal and less vineyard-oriented.
Desert peninsula with sparse settlement, some viticulture, and irrigated agriculture in river/groundwater oases.
Hot arid grazing-and-irrigation state with mining history; generally hotter and less ocean-cooled.
Semi-arid coastal grazing and grape/fruit irrigation make this a strong analogue; slightly less desert-extreme.
Strong mining-desert analogue; weaker livestock and irrigated-valley agriculture.
Arid interior grazing, mining, and sparse settlement fit well; colder and more continental.
Dry rangeland and irrigation-valley structure are good matches; less coastal-desert influence.
Arid ranching and irrigated valleys fit well; hotter and less cool-desert.
Dry plateau-oasis agriculture with pastoral margins resembles the structure well; far more continental.
Very strong desert-oasis analogue; too urban-oasis concentrated and less grazing-based.
Interior pastoralism and arid mining belts fit; winter-rainfall south coast broadens the state beyond the analogue.
Dry pastoral and mining country gives a good lower-tier match; geography is broader and rainfall seasonality differs.
Arid irrigated-valley viticulture plus sparse grazing uplands fit very well; more Andean and more oasis-focused.
Semi-desert grazing with oasis viticulture is a good analogue; somewhat warmer and more continental.
Desert grazing and irrigated agriculture offer a useful structural parallel; much denser and more intensively engineered.
Arid sparse pastoral province with oasis farming; less coastal-desert viticulture.
Desert basins with irrigated fruit and grazing margins fit in structure; too cold and more continental.
Extremely sparse arid rangeland and mining provide a useful low-end analogue; crop side is much weaker.
Useful mainly for vast sparsely settled pastoralism; northern tropical belts reduce the climatic fit.
